title: Design Systems
objectives: Understand a design system via a real-life analogy
Know the three layers: tokens → primitives → composites
Build a small design system with a Theme + tokens + widgets
Handle dark mode and multi-theme apps

sections.markdown:
**Think of a design system as an IKEA catalog for your app.**

- IKEA doesn't ship you a random tree and hope you build a chair. It ships you STANDARDIZED PIECES: legs of specific lengths, boards of specific widths, screws of specific gauges. Every kitchen, every bedroom, every office chair uses the same standardized pieces.
- Every IKEA piece has a **name and part number** (KALLAX shelf, POÄNG chair). You never say 'bring me a squarish wooden shelf' — you say 'bring KALLAX'.
- Contractors (feature teams) BUILD FURNITURE from these pieces. They don't cut their own wood. They don't paint their own colors. They compose.

**In code:** a design system is a set of standardized visual pieces — colors (`primary`, `surface`), spacing values (`xs=4`, `sm=8`, `md=16`), typography (`headline`, `body`, `caption`), primitive widgets (`AppButton`, `AppTextField`, `AppCard`), composite widgets (`AppEmptyState`, `AppLoadingScreen`). Every feature IMPORTS from the design system and composes. Nobody hard-codes `Colors.blue` or `EdgeInsets.all(15.5)`.

**Why this matters:** without a design system, every team invents their own shade of blue and their own button padding. The app looks 'built by 10 teams' — because it was. With a design system, the app looks COHERENT even as it grows. Onboarding new engineers takes hours instead of weeks. Designers ship faster because they have named components. Accessibility is centralized. Dark mode is one line of code, not a feature-by-feature refactor.

**Every serious product Flutter app has a design system.** Not having one is a decision — usually accidental — that costs a year of tech debt down the road.

**A design system** is a shared set of visual + behavioral standards + reusable components — packaged as code — that every feature of an app depends on. It has three layers: **(1) Tokens** — the atoms: colors, spacing scale, font sizes, radii, elevation. **(2) Primitives** — the widgets: `AppButton`, `AppTextField`, `AppCard`, `AppIcon`. **(3) Composites** — the patterns: `AppEmptyState`, `AppLoadingIndicator`, `AppErrorScreen`. Together they enforce visual consistency and let features move fast.
## The three layers

rows: Named values — colors, spacing, radius, typography
Compositions of primitives for common UI patterns

sections.markdown: ## Sample program — a minimal design system
## Common mistakes

**Mistake 1: Hard-coded values in features.** `EdgeInsets.all(15.5)`, `Colors.blue`. Every feature invents its own scale → visual chaos. Fix: never a raw number or color outside `AppSpacing` / `AppColors`.

**Mistake 2: One giant primitive.** `AppButton` with 20 boolean props (`isPrimary`, `isSecondary`, `isGhost`, `isLoading`, `isIcon`, `isCompact` ...). Use a `variant:` enum or split into related widgets.
**Mistake 3: Skipping the token layer.** Widgets hard-code colors internally → dark mode is a nightmare. Every value comes from a token.
**Mistake 4: No composite patterns.** Every feature reinvents the empty state, the error banner, the loading spinner. Extract composites once used 3+ times.

**Mistake 5: Design system as a personal project.** One engineer builds it in isolation; other engineers ignore it; drift. The design system must be OWNED, DOCUMENTED, ADOPTED — usually with a designer partner.
## Best practices
**1. Tokens are the single source of truth.** Colors, spacing, typography, radii — all named. No magic numbers.
**2. Variants over booleans.** `variant: AppButtonVariant.primary` beats `isPrimary: true`.
**3. Composites for repeat patterns.** Empty state, error banner, loading screen — extract once used 3+ times.
**4. Match Material 3 (or Cupertino) primitives.** Don't reinvent scroll physics; wrap Flutter's Material widgets with your tokens.
**5. Ship a docs playground.** A screen listing every primitive + composite with example usages. Devs and designers reference it.
**6. Dark mode from day 1.** Design tokens for both. Don't retrofit later.
**7. Move to its own package** when 3+ features consume it. Enforces boundary.
## Practice programs
**Practice 1 (Easy) — Extract tokens.** Move every color and spacing in your app into `AppColors` + `AppSpacing`. Watch consistency emerge.
**Practice 2 (Medium) — AppButton primitive.** With variants (primary/secondary/ghost), loading state, optional icon. Use it in 3 screens.
**Practice 3 (Medium) — AppEmptyState composite.** Icon + title + message + optional action. Replace all raw empty-state implementations.
**Practice 4 (Hard) — Dark mode.** Add dark tokens; wire `ThemeMode.system`; verify entire app switches.
**Practice 5 (Hard) — Docs playground screen.** List every primitive with 3-5 example configurations. Devs use it as reference.
**Practice 6 (Expert) — Extract to its own package.** `core_ui` package; features depend on it; enforce no feature imports another.
## Interview Q&A — with full answers you can quote

**Q1: What is a design system?**

**A:** A design system is a shared set of visual + behavioral standards + reusable components — packaged as code — that every feature depends on. Three layers: **(1) Tokens** — the atoms: colors, spacing, typography, radii. **(2) Primitives** — the building blocks: `AppButton`, `AppTextField`, `AppCard`. **(3) Composites** — the patterns: `AppEmptyState`, `AppLoadingScreen`. Together they enforce visual consistency, speed up feature development, centralize accessibility, and make dark mode / theming trivial. Every serious product Flutter app has one; not having one is usually an accidental decision with year-long consequences.

**Q2: What are design tokens?**

**A:** Named values that atomize every visual property of the app. `AppColors.primary`, `AppSpacing.md`, `AppRadii.lg`, `AppTypography.headline`. **Why:** (1) NEVER hard-code `Colors.blue` or `EdgeInsets.all(15.5)`. (2) Dark mode = swap the token values, all primitives auto-update. (3) A designer can rename a color once and every screen changes. (4) A11y contrast fixes are centralized. **Best practice:** every widget the app uses reads tokens, not raw values. Grep for `Color(0xff` in your feature code — if that returns anything, you've bypassed the token system. **Sources of tokens:** Figma → Style Dictionary → generated `.dart` file, or hand-written like the sample.

**Q3: What's the difference between a primitive and a composite?**

**A:** **Primitives** are BUILDING BLOCKS — the design-system version of `Text`, `Container`, `Button`. `AppButton`, `AppTextField`, `AppIcon`, `AppCard`. Small, focused, general-purpose. Feature teams compose them into screens. **Composites** are common PATTERNS made of primitives — reused UI shapes: `AppEmptyState` (icon + title + message + action), `AppLoadingIndicator`, `AppErrorScreen`, `AppSectionHeader`, `AppLabelValueRow`. When a specific composition appears in 3+ screens, extract it as a composite. Rule: primitives are visual atoms; composites are shape-of-UI patterns.

**Q4: How do you handle dark mode in a design system?**

**A:** Two-token approach: **(1) Semantic tokens** — `AppColors.surface`, `AppColors.textPrimary`, `AppColors.error`. Named by purpose, not by color. **(2) Two theme values per semantic** — one for light, one for dark. `Theme.of(context).colorScheme.surface` returns the RIGHT color for the current mode. Widgets NEVER hard-code hex or `Colors.X`; they read `Theme.of(context).colorScheme.*`. Wire `ThemeMode.system` in `MaterialApp` so the OS toggle just works. **Rule:** never a color literal in a widget. If you find `Color(0xff...)` outside the tokens file, that's the bug — even 'gray' has to come from a token.

**Q5: When should you extract a widget into the design system vs keep it in a feature?**

**A:** **Rule of three.** First use → inline in the feature. Second use → copy-paste. Third use → extract to design system. Preemptive extraction bloats the design system into a dumping ground; late extraction leaves duplicated code. **Signals to extract:** (1) 3+ features have the widget. (2) The widget represents a REUSABLE PATTERN (empty state, loader). (3) A designer explicitly names it as a design-system component. **Signals to keep in-feature:** (1) It's tightly coupled to the feature's business logic. (2) It uses feature-specific data models. (3) It has never been reused. When in doubt, keep it local; extract on demand.

**Q6: How do you organize a design system package?**

**A:** As a package (`core_ui`) with three folders: **`lib/tokens/`** — `colors.dart`, `spacing.dart`, `radii.dart`, `typography.dart`. **`lib/theme/`** — `light_theme.dart`, `dark_theme.dart`, `AppTheme` extending `ThemeExtension` for custom tokens. **`lib/widgets/`** — `AppButton`, `AppTextField`, `AppCard`, `AppEmptyState`, `AppLoadingIndicator`. Public API in `lib/core_ui.dart` re-exports everything. **`example/` or `lib/playground/`** — a screen listing every widget with usages; devs and designers reference it. If the app is modular, `core_ui` is a Dart package under `packages/`; features depend on it, never on each other.

**Q7: What are common design-system anti-patterns?**

**A:** **(1) Skipping tokens** — widgets hard-code colors → dark mode is a rewrite. **(2) Prop soup** — `AppButton` with 20 booleans (isPrimary, isSecondary, isGhost, isCompact, ...). Use variants. **(3) One giant widget** — `AppScreen` doing every layout job. Split into focused widgets. **(4) No documentation / playground** — devs don't know what's available; they reinvent instead of reuse. **(5) Design system in isolation** — an engineer's side project ignored by others. Needs OWNERSHIP, docs, adoption process. **(6) Overreach** — turning every feature widget into a 'reusable' component. Rule of three prevents this. **(7) No dark mode / a11y from day one** — huge retrofits later.

## Real-world usage

- **Every serious product Flutter app** has a design system, whether formally or informally.
- **Google's Material 3** — a design system that Flutter ships as a first-class widget set.
- **Airbnb's DLS** (Design Language System) — the reference SDUI + DS combo.
- **Uber Base** — cross-platform (Web + iOS + Android + Flutter) DS.
- **IBM Carbon** — enterprise DS ported to Flutter.
- **Widgetbook / storybook_flutter** — playground / catalog tooling.
- **Style Dictionary / Tokens Studio** — Figma → code token pipelines.
- **Smart Consumer** — likely has an implicit DS via `AppTheme`; formalizing it is a next step.

## Advanced notes

- **`ThemeExtension`** — custom theme tokens beyond Material's `ColorScheme`.
- **Style Dictionary** — Amazon's tool for generating tokens from a canonical source.
- **Tokens Studio for Figma** — sync design tokens between Figma and code.
- **`widgetbook`** — a Flutter-native storybook / component catalog.
- **A11y in a design system** — semantic labels baked in, minimum tap-target sizes, contrast checks.
- **Golden tests per component** — snapshot every widget in every variant + theme.
- **Package-per-designer** — very large orgs give each product team a subpackage that extends the core DS.
- **DS versioning** — semver; breaking changes get their own major.
- **Adoption metrics** — % of features using DS primitives; % of screens with only DS composites.
- **DS governance** — an RFC process for adding new tokens or primitives.
- **Multi-brand support** — the DS accepts a `Brand` theme; primitives adapt automatically.
- **Runtime theming** — `ChangeNotifier`-backed `AppTheme` that features observe; hot-swap themes.

flashcards.back: Extract to the design system after the third use. Not before.
Colors, spacings, radii. Always read from tokens.
Two-token approach. Semantic names (surface, textPrimary). Two values per semantic.
A screen listing every DS widget with usages. Widgetbook is the popular tool.

options: After 3+ features use the widget (rule of three)
Essential — devs reference it instead of reinventing

challenges.text: Extract every color/spacing from your app into AppColors + AppSpacing.
Grep for Color(0xff and EdgeInsets.all outside tokens — reduce to zero.
AppButton primitive with 3 variants + loading + icon.
AppEmptyState composite; replace all raw implementations.
Wire dark mode with semantic tokens; verify entire app switches.
Build a widgetbook / playground screen listing every DS widget with examples.
Extract the DS to its own package (core_ui); enforce features depend only on it.

interviewQuestions: What's the difference between a primitive and a composite?
How do you handle dark mode in a design system?
When should you extract a widget into the design system vs keep it in a feature?
How do you organize a design system package?
